Bitcoin as a decentralized digital curre.canonical: Bitcoin as a decentralized digital currency without intermediaries
Bitcoin as a decentralized digital curre.summary: Bitcoin was introduced as a peer-to-peer electronic cash system designed to let online payments move directly between users without relying on a financial institution. It operates through a decentralized network that records and verifies transactions on a shared ledger rather than through a central bank or single administrator. In the United States, regulators do not treat Bitcoin as sovereign money: the CFTC has described virtual currencies including Bitcoin as commodities, and the IRS treats virtual currency as property for federal tax purposes.
keyPoints: Satoshi Nakamoto’s Bitcoin whitepaper describes Bitcoin as a "peer-to-peer electronic cash system" intended to enable payments without going through a financial institution.

Bitcoin’s decentralization means no central bank, company, or single administrator issues or controls the network; transaction validation is performed by distributed network participants using the blockchain.
The U.S. CFTC has stated that virtual currencies such as Bitcoin are commodities under the Commodity Exchange Act, not centrally issued currency.
The IRS says virtual currency is treated as property for federal income tax purposes, so Bitcoin transactions can trigger tax consequences such as gains or losses.

sources.title: Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System | Bitcoin Paper
Frequently asked questions on virtual currency transactions | Internal Revenue Service
CFTC Launches Virtual Currency Resource Web Page | CFTC
Bitcoin and Beyond

Bitcoin was created in 2008 by the pseud.canonical: Bitcoin was created in 2008 by the pseudonymous Satoshi Nakamoto
Bitcoin was created in 2008 by the pseud.summary: Search results support the core claim with an important date distinction: Satoshi Nakamoto, a pseudonym for the unknown person or group behind Bitcoin, published the Bitcoin white paper in 2008, while the Bitcoin network and first software launch followed in January 2009. So it is accurate to say Bitcoin was proposed in 2008 by the pseudonymous Satoshi Nakamoto, but the operational system began in 2009.
keyPoints: Britannica states that Satoshi Nakamoto is the presumed pseudonym of the person or persons who introduced Bitcoin through the 2008 paper “Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System.”
Cato Institute says the concept of Bitcoin was first made public in a 2008 paper by the pseudonymous Satoshi Nakamoto, and that the first client software appeared the following year.
Multiple sources distinguish proposal from launch: Britannica Money describes Bitcoin as created in 2009, reflecting the release of the software and network after the 2008 white paper.
Sources on the genesis block place Bitcoin’s first block on January 3, 2009, confirming that 2008 refers to the white paper/concept stage rather than the live network.

Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash.canonical: Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System
Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash.summary: "Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System" is Satoshi Nakamoto’s whitepaper, dated October 31, 2008, that introduced Bitcoin as a decentralized electronic payment system. The paper’s core contribution is a method for preventing double-spending without a trusted intermediary by combining digital signatures, a peer-to-peer network, timestamped blocks, and hash-based proof-of-work; nodes follow the longest valid proof-of-work chain as the authoritative transaction history.
keyPoints: The whitepaper was published on October 31, 2008, and is hosted by both Bitcoin.org and the Satoshi Nakamoto Institute.
Its abstract states that Bitcoin enables online payments to be sent directly from one party to another without going through a financial institution.
The paper proposes solving double-spending by timestamping transactions into a chain of hash-based proof-of-work blocks that are costly to rewrite.
It describes consensus operationally: transactions are broadcast, miners assemble them into blocks, proof-of-work is found, and nodes extend the longest valid chain.

The Quotable Satoshi - Nodes | Satoshi Nakamoto Institute

Bitcoin blockchain throughput.summary: The claim that the Bitcoin blockchain handles roughly 7 transactions per second (TPS) is a common ballpark estimate for Bitcoin’s base layer, derived from the protocol’s limited block space and average 10-minute block interval. Sources indicate this figure reflects older or simplified assumptions about average transaction size and pre-SegWit conditions; actual on-chain throughput varies with transaction composition, SegWit usage, and batching, so real-world throughput can be lower or somewhat higher than 7 TPS.
keyPoints: Bitcoin blocks are produced about every 600 seconds on average, and throughput is constrained by block capacity divided by average transaction size, which is why TPS is typically estimated rather than fixed.
Bitcoin Wiki’s scalability documentation says older and newer estimates placed pre-SegWit Bitcoin at about 7 TPS, while also noting SegWit changed effective block capacity.
A Bitcoin Wiki page on maximum transaction rate says the commonly quoted 7 tx/s figure is a ballpark approximation, and that real-world rates depend on transaction sizes and usage patterns.

An academic paper on Bitcoin throughput states that 7 TPS was the previous best-known bound for average transaction throughput, while calculating a higher theoretical upper bound under ideal assumptions.

sources.title: How many transactions per second can bitcoin really handle ? Theoretically.
Scalability FAQ - Bitcoin Wiki
Maximum transaction rate - Bitcoin Wiki
Rapido: A Layer2 Payment System for Decentralized Currencies
Systematic Literature Review of Challenges in Blockchain Scalability

Hash-linked immutable chain.summary: A hash-linked immutable chain is the core data structure behind blockchain: each block stores its own data plus a cryptographic hash pointer to the previous block, so changing an earlier block changes its hash and breaks every later link. This makes the ledger tamper-evident, and in real blockchains immutability is strengthened further by distributed validation and consensus, not by hashing alone.
keyPoints: NIST defines blockchain as a ledger where each block is cryptographically linked to the previous one after validation and consensus, making tampering evident.
Wikipedia’s technical description notes that a block typically contains the previous block’s cryptographic hash, a timestamp, and transaction data, forming a securely linked chain.
IBM explains that because later blocks depend on earlier block hashes, altering one block would require changing all subsequent blocks, which protects chain integrity.
The first block, often called the genesis block, is the exception: it starts the chain and therefore does not reference a prior block.

sources.title: Blockchain | NIST

Visa transaction throughput.summary: The claim that Visa handles around 24,000 transactions per second is not well supported by current official sources. Visa’s own recent materials describe VisaNet as having capacity to process more than 65,000 transaction messages per second, while newer corporate materials say the network processes up to 83,000 transaction messages per second worldwide and has stress-test capacity exceeding 65,000 messages per second. Older Visa materials cited lower figures such as more than 10,000 transaction messages per second for a data center and 47,000 for VisaNet, which helps explain why different numbers circulate.
keyPoints: Visa’s official VisaNet overview says the network can process over 65,000 transaction messages per second.
A 2024 Visa corporate article says Visa’s network processes up to 83,000 transaction messages per second worldwide and that stress-test capacity exceeds 65,000 messages per second.
An official Visa submission to the Reserve Bank of Australia states Visa annually stress-tests its global systems and that the current maximum accepted rate is 65,000 transactions per second.
The specific 24,000 TPS figure appears in secondary sources and social posts, but it was not corroborated by the official Visa sources found in search results.

Bitcoin block reward started at 50 BTC a.canonical: Bitcoin block reward started at 50 BTC and halves about every four years
Bitcoin block reward started at 50 BTC a.summary: Bitcoin’s block subsidy did start at 50 BTC per block when the network launched in 2009, and the protocol reduces that subsidy by 50% every 210,000 blocks—roughly every four years given Bitcoin’s target of about one block every 10 minutes. Search results consistently confirm that this schedule has produced successive rewards of 25 BTC in 2012, 12.5 BTC in 2016, 6.25 BTC in 2020, and 3.125 BTC after the April 2024 halving.
keyPoints: Bitcoin’s initial mining reward was 50 BTC per block at launch in 2009.
Halvings are triggered by block height, not a fixed calendar date: they occur every 210,000 blocks, which is approximately every four years.
The halving history confirmed in search results is 50→25 BTC (2012), 25→12.5 BTC (2016), 12.5→6.25 BTC (2020), and 6.25→3.125 BTC (April 2024).
This declining block reward is part of Bitcoin’s controlled supply model and works alongside the 21 million BTC maximum supply.

First miner adds the next block and earn.canonical: First miner adds the next block and earns newly minted bitcoin
First miner adds the next block and earn.summary: In Bitcoin’s proof-of-work system, miners compete to find a block hash below the network’s difficulty target. A miner who finds a valid proof of work can broadcast the candidate block for validation and, if the network accepts it, that block is added to the blockchain. The successful block includes a coinbase transaction that creates the block subsidy (newly minted bitcoin) and also collects transaction fees, so the idea is broadly correct but more precisely the reward is paid to the accepted block’s coinbase output, not simply to whoever was “first” in isolation.
keyPoints: Bitcoin mining works by repeatedly hashing a candidate block header until a hash is found below the target threshold; the network adjusts difficulty so a new block is found about every 10 minutes on average.
When a miner finds a valid hash, the completed block is broadcast to the network, and nodes independently verify it before accepting it into the blockchain.
Miner revenue comes from two parts: the block subsidy created in the block’s coinbase transaction and the transaction fees from transactions included in that block.
As of the current post-2024 halving era, the block subsidy is 3.125 BTC per block, and it halves every 210,000 blocks.

sources.title: Mining — Bitcoin

Ethereum proposal and launch timeline.summary: Ethereum was first proposed by Vitalik Buterin in late 2013 in a white paper describing a general-purpose blockchain for decentralized applications and smart contracts. After development through 2014 and early 2015, including a public crowdsale in 2014, the network officially launched as the Frontier release on July 30, 2015.
keyPoints: Vitalik Buterin first described Ethereum in a white paper in November/late 2013, positioning it as a blockchain platform for decentralized applications rather than only digital money.
The Ethereum project was developed before launch through multiple prototypes and was supported by a 2014 public crowdsale that raised about $18 million in bitcoin.
Ethereum’s first live mainnet release, called Frontier, launched on July 30, 2015, marking the creation of the genesis block and the start of the live network.
Ethereum.org describes the gap from the initial idea to official launch as about 20 months, spanning from late 2013 to mid-2015.

sources.title: Ethereum Whitepaper | ethereum.org

History of Ethereum: founder, launch and ownership | ethereum.org
Ethereum
Who Created the Ethereum Ecosystem?

Smart contract.summary: A smart contract is code plus data deployed to a blockchain and executed by the network’s nodes, which must reach the same result and record that result on-chain. In practice, smart contracts are self-executing blockchain programs that automatically run predefined logic when specified conditions are met, commonly supporting decentralized applications and digital asset transfers.
keyPoints: NIST defines a smart contract as a collection of code and data deployed through cryptographically signed blockchain transactions; execution is performed by blockchain nodes and the results are recorded on the blockchain.
Ethereum’s documentation describes a smart contract as a program that runs on the Ethereum blockchain, reflecting the common modern use of the term for on-chain programmable logic.
Because execution is replicated across network nodes and recorded on an append-only ledger, smart contracts are designed to be tamper-evident and deterministic within the blockchain system.
Smart contracts are widely used to automate transfers, application logic, and other rules without relying on a centralized intermediary, though they execute only the logic encoded in their code.

Ethereum energy consumption reduction af.canonical: Ethereum energy consumption reduction after The Merge
Ethereum energy consumption reduction af.summary: Official Ethereum documentation states that The Merge, executed on September 15, 2022, completed Ethereum’s transition from proof-of-work to proof-of-stake and reduced the network’s energy consumption by approximately 99.95%. Ethereum’s energy-consumption documentation further cites CCRI estimates that annualized electricity use fell by more than 99.988%, reflecting the elimination of energy-intensive mining.
keyPoints: Ethereum.org says The Merge was executed on September 15, 2022, merging Mainnet with the Beacon Chain and replacing proof-of-work with proof-of-stake.
The Merge page on Ethereum.org states Ethereum’s energy consumption dropped by an estimated ~99.95% after the transition.
Ethereum.org’s energy-consumption page cites CCRI estimates that annualized electricity consumption fell by more than 99.988% after The Merge.
Ethereum’s roadmap/vision materials describe the energy reduction as a major sustainability benefit of switching from proof-of-work to proof-of-stake.

sources.title: The Merge | ethereum.org
Ethereum Energy Consumption | ethereum.org
Ethereum vision | ethereum.org

Ethereum's transition to Proof of Stake.canonical: Ethereum's transition to Proof of Stake in The Merge
Ethereum's transition to Proof of Stake.summary: Ethereum completed The Merge on September 15, 2022, transitioning its mainnet from Proof of Work to Proof of Stake by merging the execution layer with the Beacon Chain consensus layer. The shift ended Ethereum mining on mainnet and made validators, rather than miners, responsible for block production and network consensus.
keyPoints: The Ethereum Foundation announced the mainnet merge plan in August 2022, with Bellatrix on September 6, 2022 and the Paris upgrade completing the transition when the network reached its Terminal Total Difficulty threshold.
Ethereum.org states that the Beacon Chain launched on December 1, 2020 and became Ethereum’s live consensus mechanism when The Merge finalized on September 15, 2022.
Post-Merge, Ethereum full nodes require both an execution client and a consensus client, reflecting the network’s new architecture under Proof of Stake.
Multiple contemporaneous reports describe the Merge as dramatically reducing Ethereum’s energy consumption, commonly by more than 99% compared with Proof of Work.

Decentralized finance applications enabl.canonical: Decentralized finance applications enable lending, borrowing, and trading without intermediaries
Decentralized finance applications enabl.summary: Decentralized finance (DeFi) applications are blockchain-based financial services that use smart contracts to let users lend, borrow, and trade digital assets without relying on traditional centralized intermediaries such as banks, brokers, or exchanges. In practice, DeFi apps commonly include lending protocols and decentralized exchanges, where transactions are executed by code and users typically keep control of their own wallets and assets.
keyPoints: The European Central Bank describes DeFi as financial services that remove traditional centralized intermediaries and rely on automated protocols; it specifically notes that smart contracts facilitate lending and borrowing, while decentralized exchanges execute trades without a centralized third party.

DeFi platforms are commonly built as decentralized applications (dApps) on blockchains, with smart contracts handling core functions such as collateral management, liquidity pools, and trade execution.

Examples of DeFi use cases include lending and borrowing protocols such as Aave and decentralized exchanges such as Uniswap, which enable crypto lending or token trading without intermediary custody or order-book operators.

Academic and policy sources note that DeFi often replicates traditional financial services inside crypto markets, but does so in a permissionless, programmable, and non-custodial way rather than through banks or brokers.

DeFi TVL peaked above $180 billion in la.canonical: DeFi TVL peaked above $180 billion in late 2021 and then fell sharply
DeFi TVL peaked above $180 billion in la.summary: Search results support the concept: DeFi total value locked (TVL) reached an all-time high above $180 billion in late 2021, with multiple sources placing the peak above $200 billion, and then declined sharply through 2022. CoinGecko’s 2021 year-end report says DeFi TVL rose to $234 billion in 2021, while The Block reported DeFiLlama data showing TVL exceeding $200 billion in October 2021. Later reporting shows the sector fell below $100 billion by May 2022 and was around $48 billion in 2022-related coverage, confirming a major drawdown.
keyPoints: The Block reported on October 13, 2021 that, according to DefiLlama, DeFi TVL had exceeded $200 billion, confirming that late-2021 TVL was well above the $180 billion threshold.

CoinGecko’s 2021 Year End Cryptocurrency Report states that overall TVL in DeFi protocols rose to $234 billion in 2021, indicating the cycle peak was in the low-$200 billions rather than merely $180 billion.

The Block reported in March 2024 that DeFi TVL had just climbed back above $100 billion for the first time since May 11, 2022, implying the post-2021 collapse took TVL below $100 billion for an extended period.

A 2023 The Block report cited about $48 billion in total DeFi TVL, and Chainalysis described TVL as having experienced a sharp decrease in the middle of 2022, consistent with the broader crypto downturn and major failures such as Terra/UST.

Total value locked (TVL) in DeFi protoco.canonical: Total value locked (TVL) in DeFi protocols
Total value locked (TVL) in DeFi protoco.summary: Total value locked (TVL) in DeFi is the total market value, usually expressed in USD, of cryptoassets deposited in a protocol’s smart contracts or aggregated across protocols and chains. It is commonly used as a size and activity metric for DeFi, similar in spirit to assets under management, but methodology matters because some trackers exclude protocol-issued tokens or non-productive deposits and TVL can be overstated by double counting across nested protocols.
keyPoints: DeFi Pulse defines TVL as the total value of assets deposited in a DeFi protocol that are generating economic activity such as lending, borrowing, liquidity provision, asset management, or insurance.
DefiLlama defines protocol TVL as the value of all coins held in a protocol’s smart contracts, and chain TVL as the sum of protocol TVL on that chain.
TVL is typically calculated by summing token balances locked in smart contracts and converting them to current USD prices, which enables cross-protocol comparison.
Research and industry sources note an important limitation: TVL can overstate economic exposure because the same underlying assets may be counted multiple times across protocols or wrappers.

Uniswap cumulative trading volume over $.canonical: Uniswap cumulative trading volume over $1.8 trillion
Uniswap cumulative trading volume over $.summary: The claim is directionally correct but outdated. Official Uniswap sources show the protocol had surpassed $1.8 trillion in cumulative trading volume by 2023, and has since grown well beyond that level. Uniswap’s own materials report over $2 trillion in all-time volume in 2023, over $2.75 trillion across v2 and v3 by January 2025, and over $3 trillion total to date on its corporate site.
keyPoints: A Uniswap blog post from 2023 said the protocol had processed over $2 trillion in all-time trading volume, confirming that the '$1.8 trillion since launch' threshold had already been exceeded by then.
Uniswap’s January 31, 2025 v4 launch announcement states that Uniswap v2 and v3 alone had processed over $2.75 trillion in trading volume.
The Uniswap Labs 'About' page says the Uniswap Protocol has processed over $3 trillion in trading volume to date, indicating the original $1.8 trillion figure is no longer current.
Historical reporting from 2022 shows Uniswap crossed $1 trillion in cumulative trading volume roughly four years after its 2018 launch, which supports the broader growth trajectory behind the claim.

Automated market maker model.summary: An automated market maker (AMM) is a decentralized exchange mechanism that prices trades with a formula applied to assets held in smart-contract liquidity pools instead of matching buyers and sellers through an order book. In the common AMM model, liquidity providers deposit a token pair into a pool, receive LP tokens or another record of pool share, and earn a portion of swap fees while traders swap against the pool and move its asset ratio and price.
keyPoints: AMMs use pooled assets in smart contracts rather than traditional order books; Uniswap describes pools as the core trading mechanism, with users supplying liquidity to enable swaps.
In the standard two-token pool model, liquidity providers deposit both assets of the pair into the pool and receive a claim representing their proportional share of the pool and fees.
Prices are adjusted algorithmically from pool reserves; a widely used constant-product form keeps the product of reserves in relation as trades add one asset and remove the other.

The model generalizes beyond simple 50/50 token pairs: Balancer-style AMMs support pools with multiple assets and custom weights, showing that AMMs are a broader class of formula-based liquidity mechanisms.

Uniswap launch in 2018.summary: Uniswap launched in 2018 as the first version of the Uniswap Protocol (v1), created by Hayden Adams. Official Uniswap sources describe it as a decentralized automated market maker on Ethereum that introduced peer-to-peer token swapping without intermediaries, with v1 launching specifically in November 2018.
keyPoints: Uniswap Labs states that Hayden Adams created the Uniswap Protocol in 2018, introducing a new way to exchange value online using automated market makers (AMMs).
Uniswap’s official support documentation says Uniswap v1 was launched in November 2018 as the first version of the protocol.
Official Uniswap materials describe v1 as the first AMM protocol to natively pair tokens against ETH on Ethereum.
A Uniswap Labs blog post on v3 says v1 launched in November 2018 as a proof of concept for AMMs, confirming the 2018 launch timeline from another official source.

Programmatic token sales on exchanges no.canonical: Programmatic token sales on exchanges not necessarily securities transactions
Programmatic token sales on exchanges no.summary: In July 2023, Judge Analisa Torres of the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of New York held in SEC v. Ripple Labs that Ripple’s institutional XRP sales were unregistered securities transactions, but its "Programmatic Sales" on digital asset exchanges were not, on the facts presented. The court’s reasoning focused on the Howey test and the economic reality of exchange trades: buyers in blind bid/ask transactions generally could not know whether they were buying from Ripple or another seller, which weakened the SEC’s argument that those purchasers were investing money in Ripple with a reasonable expectation of profits from Ripple’s efforts. The ruling is often cited for the narrower proposition that exchange-based token sales do not automatically qualify as securities transactions; it did not establish that all secondary-market or exchange token sales are never securities transactions.
keyPoints: The Ripple decision distinguished transaction types rather than declaring XRP itself inherently a security or non-security; institutional sales were treated differently from programmatic exchange sales.

The court found Ripple’s Programmatic Sales occurred through blind bid/ask transactions on trading platforms, where purchasers generally did not know if they were buying from Ripple, a fact central to the court’s Howey analysis.

Several legal analyses note the ruling was limited: it does not mean every exchange-based token sale is outside securities law, and the opinion itself did not decide all possible secondary-market XRP transactions.

The decision became influential because it was one of the first major federal rulings to say that, under specific facts, public exchange sales of a digital token by an issuer did not constitute unregistered securities offerings.

sources.title: Ripple Labs: District Court Holds That Direct Digital Token Sales Constituted Investment Contracts Under Howey, but Other Transactions Did Not | Skadden
US Federal Court issues mixed ruling in watershed SEC action on Ripple’s XRP | Norton Rose Fulbright

SEC classification of most cryptocurrenc.canonical: SEC classification of most cryptocurrencies as securities
SEC classification of most cryptocurrenc.summary: Official SEC materials do not state that all or every cryptocurrency is a security. Rather, the SEC has consistently said the classification depends on facts and circumstances under federal securities law, especially the Howey investment-contract test, while senior SEC officials have repeatedly stated that the vast majority or most crypto tokens likely are securities. The SEC’s position has been expressed through the 2017 DAO Report, the 2019 digital-asset framework, speeches and testimony by Chair Gary Gensler, and numerous enforcement actions involving token offerings, lending products, and crypto platforms.
keyPoints: In its July 25, 2017 DAO Report, the SEC said digital assets may be securities and concluded DAO tokens were securities, emphasizing that the analysis turns on the economic realities and facts and circumstances of each transaction.

In its April 3, 2019 Framework for “Investment Contract” Analysis of Digital Assets, SEC staff said a digital asset should be analyzed under the Howey test and noted that offers, sales, resales, and related activities may fall under securities laws when purchasers expect profits from others’ efforts.

Chair Gary Gensler publicly stated on September 8, 2022 that of the nearly 10,000 tokens in the crypto market, he believed the vast majority are securities, and he reiterated on September 27, 2023 that the vast majority of crypto tokens likely meet the investment-contract test.

SEC enforcement has applied this view to multiple crypto products and intermediaries, including actions against Genesis and Gemini over the Gemini Earn program in January 2023 and TradeStation Crypto over an interest-bearing lending product in February 2024; the SEC’s crypto enforcement page also lists many additional crypto-related cases.

Bitcoin and Ethereum as notable exceptio.canonical: Bitcoin and Ethereum as notable exceptions
Bitcoin and Ethereum as notable exceptio.summary: Search results support the concept that Bitcoin and Ethereum have long been treated as notable exceptions in U.S. securities-classification discussions, but only in a limited, non-absolute sense. A 2018 SEC speech by William Hinman said that, based on Ether’s then-current decentralized state, current offers and sales of Ether were not securities transactions, and multiple SEC-filed disclosures later summarized Bitcoin and Ethereum as the only digital assets senior SEC officials had publicly said were unlikely to be securities. At the same time, those disclosures stress that such statements were not binding SEC rules or court holdings, and later SEC filings continue to note that asset classification remains facts-and-circumstances dependent.
keyPoints: In a June 14, 2018 SEC speech, William Hinman stated that, aside from Ether’s original fundraising, the then-current offers and sales of Ether were not securities transactions because of the network’s decentralized structure.

Multiple SEC-filed prospectuses and disclosures from 2021-2025 repeat that, except for a few staff no-action situations involving centrally issued tokens, Bitcoin and Ethereum were the only digital assets senior SEC officials had publicly indicated were unlikely to be securities.
Those same SEC-related disclosures explicitly caution that these official statements were non-binding speaker views, not formal SEC policy, and cannot be generalized to other digital assets.

CFTC materials and enforcement documents separately treat Bitcoin and Ether as commodities or digital asset commodities, reinforcing why they are often discussed as exceptions compared with many other crypto assets.

sources.title: SEC.gov | Digital Asset Transactions: When Howey Met Gary (Plastic)
SEC filing excerpt on digital assets and securities treatment
Digital Assets Primer
Statement of Commissioner Caroline D. Pham Regarding NFA Rule on Spot Digital Asset Commodity Activities | CFTC

SEC v. Ripple.summary: In its July 13, 2023 summary-judgment ruling in SEC v. Ripple Labs, the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of New York held that Ripple’s institutional XRP sales were unregistered securities offerings, but its "programmatic" sales on digital-asset exchanges were not. The court reasoned that programmatic buyers were in blind bid/ask transactions and could not know whether they were buying from Ripple, so the SEC failed to establish the third Howey prong for those sales. The SEC’s 2020 complaint had alleged Ripple used both institutional and market/programmatic XRP sales to raise funds. Later proceedings produced an August 7, 2024 final judgment imposing a $125,035,150 civil penalty, and in 2025 the SEC announced a settlement agreement under which $50 million would satisfy that penalty if approved.
keyPoints: Judge Analisa Torres’s July 13, 2023 ruling stated that Ripple’s programmatic XRP sales on exchanges "did not constitute the offer and sale of investment contracts," while institutional sales did violate Section 5.

The court emphasized that programmatic sales were blind bid/ask transactions: buyers could not know whether their money went to Ripple or another XRP seller, undermining a reasonable expectation of profits from Ripple’s efforts.
The SEC’s December 22, 2020 complaint alleged Ripple’s market sales depended on trading algorithms and that Ripple referred to those market sales as "programmatic sales."

According to the SEC’s litigation release, the district court’s August 7, 2024 final judgment imposed a $125,035,150 civil penalty; the SEC later announced a settlement agreement calling for $50 million to satisfy that penalty, with the remainder returned to Ripple.

sources.title: Case 1:20-cv-10832-AT-SN Document 874 Filed 07/13/23 Page 1 of 34

SEC.gov | Ripple Labs, Inc., Bradley Garlinghouse, and Christian Larsen
